metadata.title: Google Professional Cloud Architect PCA Practice Questions

1.name: Designing and Planning a Cloud Solution Architecture

4.name: Analyzing and Optimizing Technical and Business Processes

questions.question: A company wants to host a web application that automatically scales based on traffic without managing underlying servers. Which Google Cloud service is BEST suited?
options: App Engine Standard Environment
Compute Engine with manual scaling
Cloud Functions
Bare Metal Solution
questions.explanation: App Engine Standard Environment provides fully managed auto-scaling for web applications without requiring server management. Cloud Functions is better for event-driven functions, not full web apps. Compute Engine requires manual server management.

questions.question: Which compute service allows you to run containers without managing the underlying cluster infrastructure?
options: Cloud Run
GKE Standard mode
Compute Engine
App Engine Flexible
questions.explanation: Cloud Run is a fully managed serverless platform for running containers without managing cluster infrastructure. GKE Standard requires cluster management. Compute Engine is VM-based. App Engine Flexible runs in containers but has more constraints.

questions.question: A startup needs a relational database for their application with minimal operational overhead. They expect their data to stay under 10 GB. Which service should they choose?
options: Cloud SQL
Cloud Spanner
BigQuery
Bigtable
questions.explanation: Cloud SQL is a fully managed relational database ideal for small to medium workloads with minimal operational overhead. Cloud Spanner is designed for global-scale relational data and would be overkill. BigQuery is an analytics data warehouse. Bigtable is a NoSQL wide-column store.

questions.question: A company is designing a solution that requires a globally distributed relational database with strong consistency and 99.999% availability SLA. Which Google Cloud service should they use?
options: Cloud Spanner
Cloud SQL with read replicas
Firestore in Native mode
BigQuery
questions.explanation: Cloud Spanner provides a globally distributed, strongly consistent relational database with a 99.999% availability SLA for multi-region configurations. Cloud SQL does not offer the same global consistency guarantees. Firestore is a NoSQL document database. BigQuery is an analytics warehouse.

questions.question: An application processes millions of time-series IoT events per second requiring single-digit millisecond latency for reads and writes. Which storage solution is MOST appropriate?
options: Cloud Bigtable
Cloud SQL
Cloud Spanner
Firestore
questions.explanation: Cloud Bigtable is purpose-built for high-throughput, low-latency time-series and IoT data at massive scale. Cloud SQL and Spanner are relational databases not optimized for this access pattern. Firestore has lower throughput limits.

questions.question: A retail company wants to migrate their on-premises Hadoop cluster to Google Cloud with minimal code changes. Which service should they use?
options: Dataproc
Dataflow
BigQuery
Cloud Composer
questions.explanation: Dataproc is a managed Hadoop and Spark service that allows migration of existing Hadoop workloads with minimal code changes. Dataflow uses Apache Beam (different API). BigQuery is a data warehouse. Cloud Composer orchestrates workflows but doesn't process data.

questions.question: You are designing a multi-region architecture for a critical application. Which load balancing option distributes traffic across multiple regions with automatic failover?
options: Global External HTTP(S) Load Balancer
Regional Internal TCP/UDP Load Balancer
Network Load Balancer
Regional External HTTP(S) Load Balancer
questions.explanation: The Global External HTTP(S) Load Balancer distributes traffic across multiple regions globally, provides automatic failover, and uses Google's global network for optimal routing. Regional load balancers only operate within a single region.

questions.question: A financial services company requires that all data remain within the EU. They need a multi-region setup for high availability within Europe. How should you configure the solution?
options: Use a dual-region Cloud Storage bucket with EU locations and restrict resource creation to EU regions using organization policies
Use a multi-region Cloud Storage bucket set to 'US' and replicate data to an EU bucket
Deploy all resources to a single EU zone for maximum data residency control
Use Cloud CDN to cache all data at EU edge locations
questions.explanation: A dual-region bucket within EU locations ensures data stays in the EU while providing high availability. Organization policies can restrict resource creation to EU regions. A US multi-region bucket would violate data residency. A single zone is not high availability. CDN caches at edge locations which may be outside the EU.

questions.question: You need to design a disaster recovery solution with an RPO of near zero and RTO of less than 5 minutes for a mission-critical application. Which approach should you use?
options: Hot standby with synchronous replication across regions
Warm standby with daily backups restored from Cloud Storage
Cold standby with weekly snapshots
Backup and restore from tape archives
questions.explanation: A hot standby with synchronous replication provides near-zero RPO and very low RTO as the standby environment is always running and current. Warm and cold standbys have longer RPOs and RTOs. Tape backup is the slowest recovery option.

questions.question: A company is running a monolithic application on Compute Engine. They want to modernize it using microservices. The team has limited container orchestration experience. Which migration approach is MOST appropriate?
options: Incrementally extract services and deploy to Cloud Run
Rewrite the entire application as Kubernetes microservices on GKE from scratch
Use the Strangler Fig pattern to migrate directly to Cloud Functions
Keep the monolith and add more Compute Engine instances
questions.explanation: Incrementally extracting services and deploying to Cloud Run follows the Strangler Fig pattern while using a serverless container platform that doesn't require Kubernetes expertise. A full rewrite is risky and time-consuming. Cloud Functions is for event-driven functions, not microservices. Adding more instances doesn't modernize.

questions.question: Which Google Cloud service provides a fully managed message queue for decoupling application components?
options: Cloud Pub/Sub
Cloud Tasks
Cloud Scheduler
Memorystore
questions.explanation: Cloud Pub/Sub is a fully managed messaging service for decoupling publishers and subscribers. Cloud Tasks is for task queuing with guaranteed delivery. Cloud Scheduler is a cron job service. Memorystore is an in-memory data store.

questions.question: You need to run a containerized workload that requires GPU access for machine learning inference. Which GKE feature should you configure?
options: Node pools with GPU accelerators attached
GKE Autopilot with default configuration
Preemptible VMs in a standard node pool
Cloud TPU pods connected via VPC peering
questions.explanation: GKE supports node pools with attached GPU accelerators (NVIDIA Tesla) for ML workloads. GKE Autopilot does support GPUs but requires specific configuration. Preemptible VMs do not include GPUs by default. Cloud TPU pods are separate from GKE node pools.

questions.question: An organization operates in a hybrid cloud environment. They need to manage Kubernetes clusters across on-premises and multiple cloud providers with consistent policies. Which Google Cloud solution should they use?
options: Anthos
GKE Standard
Cloud Run for Anthos
Migrate for Anthos
questions.explanation: Anthos is Google's hybrid and multi-cloud platform that enables consistent management of Kubernetes clusters across on-premises data centers and multiple cloud providers with unified policies. GKE Standard only runs on Google Cloud. Cloud Run for Anthos and Migrate for Anthos are subsets of Anthos functionality.

questions.question: You are designing a data pipeline that must process both real-time streaming data and batch historical data using the same codebase. Which architecture pattern and service combination is BEST?
options: Kappa architecture using Dataflow with Apache Beam
Lambda architecture using separate Dataproc and Dataflow pipelines
Batch-only architecture with Cloud Scheduler triggering Dataproc jobs
Real-time only architecture using Pub/Sub and Cloud Functions
questions.explanation: The Kappa architecture with Dataflow and Apache Beam allows a single codebase to handle both batch and streaming data. Apache Beam's unified model abstracts batch and stream processing. Lambda architecture requires two separate codebases. The other options handle only one processing mode.

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of using Shared VPC in a multi-project Google Cloud environment?
options: It allows resources in different projects to communicate using internal IP addresses on a shared network
It automatically encrypts all traffic between projects
It provides a dedicated physical network connection to on-premises
It eliminates the need for firewall rules between projects
questions.explanation: Shared VPC allows an organization to connect resources from multiple projects to a common VPC network, enabling internal IP communication across projects while maintaining project-level separation for billing and access control.

questions.question: A company needs to connect their on-premises data center to Google Cloud with a dedicated, private connection offering up to 100 Gbps bandwidth. Which service should they use?
options: Dedicated Interconnect
Partner Interconnect
Cloud VPN
Cloud CDN
questions.explanation: Dedicated Interconnect provides a direct physical connection between on-premises and Google Cloud with up to 100 Gbps bandwidth per link. Partner Interconnect uses a service provider and offers lower bandwidth. Cloud VPN uses encrypted tunnels over the public internet with lower bandwidth. Cloud CDN is for content delivery.

questions.question: You are architecting a solution for a gaming company that needs to serve real-time leaderboards with sub-millisecond latency to millions of concurrent users. Which combination of services would you recommend?
options: Memorystore for Redis for the leaderboard cache with Bigtable for persistent storage
Cloud SQL with read replicas for leaderboard queries
Firestore in Native mode with client-side caching
BigQuery with materialized views for leaderboard aggregations
questions.explanation: Memorystore for Redis provides sub-millisecond latency for sorted sets (ideal for leaderboards) with Bigtable as the persistent backing store for high-throughput writes. Cloud SQL would struggle at this scale. Firestore latency is not sub-millisecond. BigQuery is not designed for real-time low-latency queries.

questions.question: Which Google Cloud service should you use to run stateless containers that automatically scale to zero when not receiving requests?
options: Cloud Run
GKE Autopilot
App Engine Standard
Compute Engine Managed Instance Groups
questions.explanation: Cloud Run is a serverless platform that runs stateless containers and can scale to zero instances when there are no requests, minimizing costs. GKE Autopilot scales pods but not to zero by default. App Engine Standard can scale to zero but is not container-based. MIGs require a minimum of one instance.

questions.question: Which storage class in Cloud Storage offers the lowest cost for data that is accessed less than once a year and stored for at least 365 days?
options: Archive
Coldline
Nearline
Standard
questions.explanation: Archive storage class offers the lowest storage cost and is designed for data accessed less than once a year with a minimum 365-day storage duration. Coldline is for quarterly access, Nearline for monthly access, and Standard for frequently accessed data.

questions.question: An e-commerce company wants to deploy their application across three regions (us-central1, europe-west1, asia-east1) with automatic DNS-based failover. Users should be routed to the closest healthy region. Which combination of services should they use?
options: Global HTTP(S) Load Balancer with health checks and backend services in each region
Cloud DNS with weighted routing policy and manual failover
Three separate regional load balancers with Cloud DNS geolocation routing
Traffic Director with service mesh across all regions
questions.explanation: The Global HTTP(S) Load Balancer automatically routes users to the closest healthy backend, provides built-in health checks, and handles failover without DNS changes. DNS-based failover has TTL delays. Traffic Director is for service mesh, not external user routing.

questions.question: A company wants to migrate a legacy application from on-premises VMs to Google Cloud with minimal modifications. Which migration strategy are they using?
options: Lift and shift (Rehost)
Refactor
Replatform
Rebuild
questions.explanation: Lift and shift (Rehost) involves migrating applications to the cloud with minimal or no modifications. Refactoring involves changing the application code. Replatforming makes some optimizations. Rebuilding means rewriting from scratch.

questions.question: Which Google Cloud service is a fully managed, serverless data warehouse designed for large-scale analytics?
options: BigQuery
Cloud Spanner
Dataproc
Cloud SQL
questions.explanation: BigQuery is Google Cloud's fully managed, serverless enterprise data warehouse optimized for large-scale analytics using SQL. Cloud Spanner is an operational relational database. Dataproc runs Hadoop/Spark. Cloud SQL is for transactional relational workloads.

questions.question: You are designing a content management system that stores documents with complex hierarchical relationships. Users need to run ad-hoc queries filtering on multiple nested attributes. Which database is MOST suitable?
options: Firestore in Native mode
Cloud Bigtable
Cloud SQL (MySQL)
Memorystore for Redis
questions.explanation: Firestore in Native mode is a NoSQL document database that supports hierarchical data models with subcollections and allows querying on multiple fields with composite indexes. Bigtable is optimized for flat, wide-column data. Cloud SQL works but relational schema would require complex JOINs for hierarchical data. Memorystore is for caching.

questions.question: Which VPC networking feature allows two VPC networks to connect and communicate using internal IP addresses without going through the public internet?
options: VPC Network Peering
Cloud NAT
Cloud VPN
Cloud Router
questions.explanation: VPC Network Peering connects two VPC networks so that resources in each can communicate using internal IP addresses. Cloud NAT provides outbound internet access. Cloud VPN creates encrypted tunnels. Cloud Router manages dynamic routing.

questions.question: A development team wants to deploy a simple REST API backend without managing any infrastructure. The API receives sporadic traffic with periods of no usage. Which service is MOST cost-effective?
options: Cloud Run
GKE Standard with a single node pool
Compute Engine with an e2-micro instance
App Engine Flexible Environment
questions.explanation: Cloud Run is serverless, scales to zero, and charges only for actual request handling time, making it the most cost-effective for sporadic traffic. GKE requires always-on nodes. Compute Engine charges even when idle. App Engine Flexible has minimum instances.

questions.question: An organization needs to process streaming financial transactions with exactly-once semantics and event-time windowing. Late-arriving events must be handled correctly. Which pipeline design should they use?
options: Pub/Sub for ingestion with Dataflow using Apache Beam windowing and watermarks
Pub/Sub with Cloud Functions processing each message individually
Kafka on Compute Engine with a custom consumer application
Cloud Scheduler triggering periodic BigQuery batch loads
questions.explanation: Dataflow with Apache Beam provides exactly-once processing semantics, event-time windowing, and watermark-based late data handling. Pub/Sub ensures reliable message delivery. Cloud Functions cannot do windowed aggregations. Kafka on Compute Engine requires manual management. Batch loads do not handle streaming.

questions.question: A company needs to store 50 PB of unstructured data (images and videos) at the lowest possible cost. The data is accessed approximately once per quarter for compliance audits. Which Cloud Storage configuration should they use?
options: Coldline storage class in a multi-region bucket
Archive storage class in a regional bucket
Standard storage class in a dual-region bucket
Nearline storage class in a regional bucket
questions.explanation: Coldline storage is designed for data accessed approximately once per quarter (90 days), making it ideal for quarterly compliance audits. Multi-region provides redundancy. Archive has a 365-day minimum and is for less-than-annual access. Standard is too expensive for infrequent access. Nearline is for monthly access.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Cloud NAT in Google Cloud networking?
options: It allows VMs without external IP addresses to access the internet for outbound connections
It provides DNS resolution for internal resources
It encrypts traffic between VPC networks
It load balances traffic across multiple regions
questions.explanation: Cloud NAT (Network Address Translation) enables VMs without external IP addresses to make outbound connections to the internet while preventing inbound connections from the internet, improving security posture.

questions.question: A healthcare company needs to migrate a legacy Oracle database application to Google Cloud. The application uses Oracle-specific PL/SQL procedures and cannot be rewritten in the short term. What is the BEST migration strategy?
options: Use Bare Metal Solution to run Oracle Database on dedicated hardware connected to Google Cloud
Migrate the database to Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL and rewrite all PL/SQL procedures
Use Datastream to replicate data to BigQuery and point the application to BigQuery
Run Oracle in a Compute Engine VM with a standard machine type
questions.explanation: Bare Metal Solution provides dedicated hardware for running Oracle Database with direct connectivity to Google Cloud services, preserving Oracle-specific features like PL/SQL. Migrating to PostgreSQL requires rewriting procedures. BigQuery is not an OLTP database. Running Oracle on standard VMs may violate Oracle licensing.

questions.question: Which Google Cloud service provides a managed Apache Kafka-compatible streaming platform?
options: Managed Service for Apache Kafka
Cloud Pub/Sub
Dataflow
Cloud Composer
questions.explanation: Google Cloud's Managed Service for Apache Kafka provides a fully managed, Apache Kafka-compatible streaming service. Pub/Sub is Google's native messaging service (not Kafka-compatible). Dataflow processes streams but is not a message broker. Cloud Composer orchestrates workflows.

questions.question: In GKE, what is the primary difference between Autopilot and Standard mode?
options: Autopilot manages the nodes automatically while Standard mode requires you to manage node pools
Autopilot uses VMs while Standard mode uses containers
Autopilot is free while Standard mode requires payment
Autopilot only supports stateless workloads while Standard supports all workloads
questions.explanation: GKE Autopilot is a fully managed mode where Google manages the underlying node infrastructure, including node pools, scaling, and security patches. Standard mode gives you full control over node pool configuration but requires manual management.

questions.question: Which Compute Engine feature allows you to save costs by using unused capacity at a significant discount, but instances can be preempted with 30 seconds notice?
options: Spot VMs
Committed Use Discounts
Sustained Use Discounts
Sole-tenant nodes
questions.explanation: Spot VMs (formerly Preemptible VMs) offer up to 91% discount on Compute Engine pricing by using excess capacity. They can be reclaimed by Google with a 30-second warning. Committed Use Discounts require a 1-3 year commitment. Sustained Use Discounts apply automatically for consistent usage.

questions.question: You need to provision a group of identical VMs that automatically replaces unhealthy instances and supports rolling updates. Which Compute Engine feature should you use?
options: Managed Instance Group (MIG)
Unmanaged Instance Group
Instance Template only
Sole-tenant node group
questions.explanation: Managed Instance Groups (MIGs) automatically manage identical VM instances, including auto-healing (replacing unhealthy instances), auto-scaling, and rolling update support. Unmanaged groups don't offer these features. Instance templates define VM config but don't manage instances.

questions.question: A company needs to store relational data in PostgreSQL with automatic failover and read replicas. Which configuration provides the highest availability?
options: Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL with a regional HA configuration and cross-region read replicas
PostgreSQL installed on a single Compute Engine VM with local SSD
Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL with a zonal instance and automated backups
AlloyDB for PostgreSQL with a single primary instance
questions.explanation: Cloud SQL regional HA configuration provides automatic failover between zones within a region, and cross-region read replicas provide additional geographic redundancy. A single VM has no failover. Zonal instances are vulnerable to zone outages. A single primary AlloyDB without read pools has limited availability.

questions.question: You are configuring a Cloud Spanner instance for a global banking application. You need to minimize write latency for users in North America while maintaining read access for users in Europe and Asia. How should you configure the instance?
options: Use a multi-region configuration with the leader region in North America
Deploy separate single-region instances in each continent with application-level replication
Use a single-region instance in us-central1 with global load balancing
Deploy a dual-region configuration spanning North America and Europe only
questions.explanation: Cloud Spanner multi-region configurations allow you to specify a leader region for writes (North America for lowest write latency) while automatically replicating data to other regions for low-latency reads. Separate instances lose consistency. Single-region adds latency for remote users.

questions.question: Which tool should you use to create and manage Google Cloud resources using declarative configuration files?
options: Terraform
Cloud Console
gcloud CLI (imperative commands)
Cloud Shell Editor
questions.explanation: Terraform uses declarative configuration files (HCL) to define and manage cloud infrastructure as code. Cloud Console is a web UI. gcloud CLI uses imperative commands. Cloud Shell Editor is a code editor.

questions.question: You need to configure a Compute Engine VM that requires a specific minimum number of CPUs and memory but want Google Cloud to select the most cost-efficient machine type. Which approach should you use?
options: Use a custom machine type specifying exact vCPU and memory requirements
Use the largest predefined machine type available
Use an N1 standard machine type with the most vCPUs
Use Sole-tenant nodes for dedicated hardware
questions.explanation: Custom machine types let you specify exact vCPU and memory, so you only pay for what you need rather than over-provisioning with a predefined type. The largest predefined type wastes resources. Sole-tenant nodes are for licensing or compliance requirements.

questions.question: A company runs batch processing jobs that take 6 hours to complete. The jobs are fault-tolerant and can be checkpointed. They want to minimize compute costs. Which configuration is MOST cost-effective?
options: Spot VMs with checkpointing enabled and automatic restart policies
On-demand Compute Engine instances with sustained use discounts
Committed use discounts with 1-year commitment
App Engine Flexible Environment with autoscaling
questions.explanation: Spot VMs offer up to 91% discount and are ideal for fault-tolerant batch jobs that support checkpointing. If preempted, the job can resume from the last checkpoint. On-demand pricing is more expensive. Committed use requires consistent long-term usage. App Engine is not designed for batch processing.

questions.question: Which Cloud Storage feature allows you to automatically transition objects to cheaper storage classes based on age?
options: Object Lifecycle Management policies
Object Versioning
Retention policies
Cloud Storage Transfer Service
questions.explanation: Object Lifecycle Management policies can automatically transition objects to cheaper storage classes (e.g., Standard to Nearline to Coldline) based on conditions like age. Versioning keeps old versions. Retention policies prevent deletion. Transfer Service moves data between sources.

questions.question: You are managing a GKE cluster that runs both production and development workloads. You need to ensure production pods always have guaranteed CPU and memory resources even when the cluster is under high load. What should you configure?
options: Resource requests and limits on pods with PriorityClasses to prioritize production workloads
Separate node pools with taints and tolerations for production only
Horizontal Pod Autoscaler for production deployments only
Vertical Pod Autoscaler for all workloads
questions.explanation: Resource requests guarantee minimum CPU/memory for pods, resource limits cap usage, and PriorityClasses ensure production pods are scheduled first and dev pods are evicted under resource pressure. Node pool separation works but wastes resources. HPA scales pods but doesn't guarantee resources. VPA adjusts resources but doesn't prioritize workloads.

questions.question: Which Compute Engine machine type family is optimized for memory-intensive workloads such as in-memory databases?
options: M-series (memory-optimized)
E-series (cost-optimized)
C-series (compute-optimized)
N-series (general-purpose)
questions.explanation: M-series machine types (like M2 and M3) are memory-optimized, offering the highest memory-to-vCPU ratios for workloads like SAP HANA and in-memory databases. E-series is cost-optimized for general workloads. C-series is compute-optimized. N-series is general-purpose.

questions.question: You need to set up a Cloud SQL instance that can automatically failover to another zone in case of a zone outage. Which feature should you enable?
options: High Availability (HA) configuration with a regional instance
Read replicas in the same zone
Point-in-time recovery
Cross-region replication
questions.explanation: Cloud SQL HA configuration creates a standby instance in a different zone within the same region. If the primary zone fails, it automatically fails over to the standby. Read replicas don't provide automatic failover. Point-in-time recovery is for data recovery, not availability. Cross-region replication provides disaster recovery, not zonal failover.

questions.question: What is the purpose of an instance template in Compute Engine?
options: It defines the machine type, disk, network, and other properties used to create VM instances in a managed instance group
It provides a backup of a running VM instance
It creates a snapshot of the VM's persistent disk
It stores the VM's boot image in Cloud Storage
questions.explanation: Instance templates define the configuration (machine type, boot disk image, network settings, etc.) for creating VM instances, particularly in managed instance groups. They are not backups, snapshots, or image storage.

questions.question: A media company needs to transcode large video files. Each transcoding job uses 32 vCPUs for approximately 2 hours. Jobs arrive unpredictably throughout the day. Which Compute Engine configuration minimizes cost while meeting SLAs?
options: Managed instance group with autoscaling using Spot VMs and an on-demand fallback
A fixed pool of 10 on-demand instances running 24/7
A single n2-highcpu-128 instance handling all jobs sequentially
Cloud Functions with maximum memory allocation
questions.explanation: A MIG with autoscaling uses Spot VMs for cost savings (up to 91% discount) and falls back to on-demand instances if Spot capacity is unavailable, ensuring SLAs are met. A fixed pool wastes money during idle periods. Sequential processing on one VM creates bottlenecks. Cloud Functions has a 60-minute timeout and limited CPU.

questions.question: Which Google Cloud database service provides PostgreSQL compatibility with automatic horizontal scaling, integrated analytics, and a columnar engine?
options: AlloyDB for PostgreSQL
Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL
Cloud Spanner
Bigtable
questions.explanation: AlloyDB for PostgreSQL is a fully managed PostgreSQL-compatible database with automatic horizontal scaling for read replicas, a built-in columnar engine for analytics, and integrated ML capabilities. Cloud SQL is simpler but lacks these advanced features. Spanner is not PostgreSQL-compatible natively. Bigtable is NoSQL.

questions.question: You are designing a GKE deployment strategy that needs to gradually shift traffic from the old version to the new version while monitoring error rates. If errors exceed a threshold, traffic should automatically roll back. Which deployment strategy should you use?
options: Canary deployment with Cloud Deploy and automated rollback based on Cloud Monitoring metrics
Blue-green deployment with manual DNS switching
Rolling update with maxUnavailable set to 0
Recreate deployment strategy
questions.explanation: Canary deployment with Cloud Deploy allows gradual traffic shifting with automated rollback based on monitoring metrics. This provides the safest deployment with automatic error detection. Blue-green requires manual switching. Rolling updates don't monitor error rates. Recreate causes downtime.

questions.question: Which feature of Compute Engine allows you to increase the size of a persistent disk without stopping the VM?
options: Online disk resize
Disk snapshots
Local SSD attachment
RAM disk creation
questions.explanation: Compute Engine persistent disks can be resized online without stopping the VM. After resizing, the file system must be extended within the OS. Snapshots create backups. Local SSDs cannot be resized. RAM disks are not persistent.

questions.question: What is the maximum size of a single Cloud SQL for MySQL instance's storage?
options: 64 TB
10 TB
1 TB
100 TB
questions.explanation: Cloud SQL for MySQL supports up to 64 TB of storage per instance. This limit applies to the total data stored in the instance including system tables and binary logs.

questions.question: You want to ensure that all Compute Engine VMs in a project are created only in specific regions. Which Google Cloud feature enforces this?
options: Organization Policy with resource location restriction constraint
IAM custom roles restricting compute.instances.create
VPC firewall rules blocking traffic from other regions
Cloud Armor security policies
questions.explanation: The Organization Policy service provides a resource location restriction constraint (constraints/gcp.resourceLocations) that enforces which regions or zones resources can be created in. IAM controls who can create resources, not where. Firewall rules control network traffic. Cloud Armor protects web applications.

questions.question: A company needs to run Windows Server workloads with specific licensing requirements that require physical server isolation. Which Compute Engine feature should they use?
options: Sole-tenant nodes
Spot VMs
Preemptible VMs
Shared-core machine types
questions.explanation: Sole-tenant nodes provide dedicated physical servers for your VMs, ensuring physical isolation. This is required for certain Windows Server licensing models (BYOL) and compliance scenarios. Spot/Preemptible VMs share hardware. Shared-core types share CPU time.

questions.question: Which type of persistent disk in Compute Engine provides the highest IOPS performance?
options: Hyperdisk Extreme
Standard persistent disk (pd-standard)
Balanced persistent disk (pd-balanced)
SSD persistent disk (pd-ssd)
questions.explanation: Hyperdisk Extreme provides the highest IOPS performance (up to 350,000 IOPS) and is designed for the most demanding database and analytics workloads. Standard disks are HDD-backed. Balanced disks are a cost-effective SSD option. SSD persistent disks offer good performance but less than Hyperdisk Extreme.

questions.question: You are using Terraform to manage GKE clusters. After applying a configuration change, you notice the cluster was destroyed and recreated instead of being updated in place. What is the likely cause?
options: A force-replacement attribute was changed that requires the resource to be recreated
Terraform always destroys and recreates GKE clusters on any change
The Terraform state file was corrupted
The GKE cluster reached its maximum update limit
questions.explanation: Certain GKE attributes (like network, subnetwork, or initial_node_count) are force-replacement attributes that require destroying and recreating the cluster when changed. Terraform doesn't always recreate resources. State corruption would cause different errors. There is no maximum update limit.

questions.question: What is the principle of least privilege in the context of Google Cloud IAM?
options: Granting users only the minimum permissions needed to perform their tasks
Giving all users viewer access by default
Using only predefined roles instead of custom roles
Assigning roles at the organization level for simplicity
questions.explanation: The principle of least privilege means granting only the minimum permissions required for a user to perform their job. This reduces the blast radius of compromised credentials and limits accidental damage.

questions.question: In the Google Cloud resource hierarchy, which level is at the top?
options: Organization
Folder
Project
Resource
questions.explanation: The Google Cloud resource hierarchy from top to bottom is: Organization > Folders > Projects > Resources. The Organization node is the root and represents a company. IAM policies applied at higher levels are inherited by lower levels.

questions.question: A security team wants to prevent any project in the organization from creating external IP addresses on Compute Engine VMs. Which approach should they use?
options: Set an Organization Policy constraint to disable VM external IP addresses
Remove the compute.instances.create permission from all users
Create a firewall rule blocking all outbound traffic
Use Cloud NAT to replace all external IPs
questions.explanation: The Organization Policy constraint 'constraints/compute.vmExternalIpAccess' can restrict or disable external IP addresses on VMs across the entire organization. Removing create permissions is too broad. Firewall rules control traffic, not IP assignment. Cloud NAT doesn't prevent external IP creation.

questions.question: Which type of IAM role provides granular permissions for specific Google Cloud services and is maintained by Google?
options: Predefined roles
Basic roles (Owner, Editor, Viewer)
Custom roles
Service agent roles
questions.explanation: Predefined roles are created and maintained by Google, providing granular access control for specific services (e.g., roles/storage.objectViewer). Basic roles are coarse-grained. Custom roles are user-defined. Service agent roles are used by Google-managed service accounts.

questions.question: A company wants to ensure that sensitive data in BigQuery cannot be accessed from outside their corporate network, even by authorized users. Which Google Cloud feature provides this protection?
options: VPC Service Controls
Cloud IAM conditional role bindings
BigQuery authorized views
Data Loss Prevention API
questions.explanation: VPC Service Controls create a security perimeter around Google Cloud services like BigQuery, restricting data access to only authorized VPC networks. IAM conditions can limit by IP but VPC-SC provides a stronger perimeter. Authorized views control query access. DLP identifies sensitive data but doesn't restrict network access.

questions.question: You need to allow a third-party SaaS application running on AWS to access Google Cloud resources without sharing long-lived service account keys. Which approach should you use?
options: Workload Identity Federation with an AWS identity provider
Create a service account and share the JSON key file securely
Set up a VPN tunnel between AWS and Google Cloud
Use OAuth 2.0 client credentials with a client secret
questions.explanation: Workload Identity Federation allows external workloads (including those on AWS) to access Google Cloud resources by exchanging their native identity tokens for short-lived Google Cloud credentials, eliminating the need for service account keys. Sharing key files creates security risks. VPN provides network connectivity, not authentication. OAuth client credentials still require managing secrets.

questions.question: An organization has hundreds of projects. They need to enforce that all Cloud Storage buckets use uniform bucket-level access and prohibit ACLs. How should they implement this?
options: Set an Organization Policy constraint for uniform bucket-level access at the organization node
Write a Cloud Function to check each bucket and remove ACLs
Train all developers to enable uniform access when creating buckets
Use a cron job to run gsutil commands checking bucket configurations
questions.explanation: The Organization Policy constraint 'constraints/storage.uniformBucketLevelAccess' enforces uniform bucket-level access across all projects in the organization, preventing ACL-based access at creation time. Cloud Functions and cron jobs are reactive, not preventive. Training is not enforceable.

questions.question: Which Google Cloud service allows you to detect and classify sensitive data like credit card numbers and social security numbers in your data stores?
options: Cloud Data Loss Prevention (DLP) API
Security Command Center
Cloud Armor
Binary Authorization
questions.explanation: Cloud DLP (Data Loss Prevention) API automatically discovers, classifies, and protects sensitive data across Google Cloud services. It can detect PII like credit card numbers, SSNs, and other sensitive information. Security Command Center manages security posture. Cloud Armor protects web apps. Binary Authorization validates container images.

questions.question: A GKE application needs to access Cloud Storage without using service account keys. Which mechanism should you use?
options: Workload Identity to map Kubernetes service accounts to Google service accounts
Mount the service account key as a Kubernetes secret
Use the default Compute Engine service account for all pods
Hardcode the API key in the application's environment variables
questions.explanation: Workload Identity is the recommended way for GKE workloads to access Google Cloud services. It maps Kubernetes service accounts to Google Cloud service accounts, providing automatic credential management without keys. Mounting keys creates security risks. Using the default SA violates least privilege. Hardcoding API keys is a security anti-pattern.

questions.question: You are designing a multi-tenant SaaS application on Google Cloud. Each tenant's data must be completely isolated, and tenants should not be able to access each other's data even if a misconfiguration occurs. Which architecture provides the strongest isolation?
options: Separate Google Cloud projects per tenant with VPC Service Controls
Shared BigQuery dataset with row-level security using IAM
Single Cloud SQL database with application-level tenant filtering
Shared GKE cluster with Kubernetes namespaces per tenant
questions.explanation: Separate projects per tenant provide the strongest isolation as each project has its own IAM, networking, and resource boundaries. VPC Service Controls add an additional security perimeter. Row-level security depends on correct policy configuration. Application-level filtering can be bypassed. Namespaces provide soft isolation only.

questions.question: Which Google Cloud IAM feature allows you to grant permissions that expire automatically after a specified time?
options: IAM Conditions with time-based expressions
Custom roles with expiration dates
Temporary service account keys
Organization Policy with time constraints
questions.explanation: IAM Conditions allow you to create time-based conditional role bindings that automatically expire. You can set conditions like 'grant access until December 31, 2026.' Custom roles don't have expiration. Service account keys don't expire automatically. Organization Policies don't have time constraints for access.

questions.question: A healthcare organization needs to encrypt data in BigQuery using keys they control and manage. Which encryption option should they use?
options: Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK) with Cloud KMS
Google-managed encryption (default)
Customer-Supplied Encryption Keys (CSEK)
Client-side encryption before loading data
questions.explanation: CMEK with Cloud KMS allows the organization to control and manage encryption keys in Cloud KMS while Google uses those keys to encrypt BigQuery data. Default encryption doesn't give key control. CSEK is not supported by BigQuery. Client-side encryption would break BigQuery query functionality.

questions.question: An application running on GKE needs to verify that only trusted container images are deployed. Which Google Cloud service should you use?
options: Binary Authorization
Container Analysis
Artifact Registry vulnerability scanning
GKE security posture dashboard
questions.explanation: Binary Authorization enforces deploy-time security policies, requiring container images to be signed by trusted attestors before they can be deployed to GKE. Container Analysis scans for vulnerabilities. Artifact Registry scanning identifies CVEs. The security dashboard provides visibility but doesn't enforce policies.

questions.question: Which Cloud Audit Log type records API calls that modify resources, such as creating a VM or changing IAM policies?
options: Admin Activity audit logs
Data Access audit logs
System Event audit logs
Policy Denied audit logs
questions.explanation: Admin Activity audit logs record API calls that modify resource configuration or metadata, such as creating VMs, changing IAM policies, or updating firewall rules. Data Access logs record data read/write operations. System Event logs record Google-initiated actions. Policy Denied logs record access denials.

questions.question: What is the recommended way to manage secrets (like database passwords and API keys) in Google Cloud applications?
options: Store them in Secret Manager and access them at runtime
Store them in environment variables on the VM
Commit them to the source code repository
Store them in a Cloud Storage bucket
questions.explanation: Secret Manager provides a secure, centralized way to store and manage secrets with versioning, access control, and audit logging. Environment variables can be exposed in logs. Source code repositories are not secure. Cloud Storage buckets lack secret-specific security features.

questions.question: You need to design a security architecture where Google Cloud APIs can only be called from within your VPC or a specific list of IP ranges, even if valid credentials are used from outside. Which service provides this capability?
options: VPC Service Controls with access levels defined in Access Context Manager
Cloud IAM with IP-based conditions
Cloud Armor with IP allowlisting
VPC firewall rules blocking external IP ranges
questions.explanation: VPC Service Controls create a security perimeter around Google Cloud APIs. Access Context Manager defines access levels based on IP ranges, device attributes, and identity. Even with valid credentials, API calls from outside the perimeter are blocked. IAM conditions are less comprehensive. Cloud Armor protects web apps, not APIs. Firewall rules don't control API access.

questions.question: Which type of service account is automatically created by Google when you enable certain APIs, and is used by Google services to access resources in your project?
options: Google-managed service account (service agent)
User-managed service account
Default service account
Cross-project service account
questions.explanation: Google-managed service accounts (service agents) are automatically created when certain APIs are enabled and are used by Google services to perform operations on your behalf. User-managed SAs are created by users. Default SAs are created per project for Compute/App Engine. Cross-project SA is not a specific type.

questions.question: Which Security Command Center tier provides vulnerability scanning, threat detection, and compliance monitoring across an entire organization?
options: Security Command Center Premium
Security Command Center Standard
Security Command Center Free
Security Command Center Basic
questions.explanation: Security Command Center Premium provides comprehensive security features including Security Health Analytics, Event Threat Detection, Container Threat Detection, Web Security Scanner, and compliance monitoring across the organization. Standard offers basic vulnerability scanning. There is no Free or Basic tier.

questions.question: A company needs to ensure that service account keys are rotated automatically and that old keys are invalidated. Which approach is BEST?
options: Eliminate service account keys entirely and use Workload Identity or Workload Identity Federation instead
Use a cron job to rotate keys every 30 days and delete old keys
Set key expiration using IAM Conditions
Use Cloud KMS to manage service account keys
questions.explanation: The best practice is to eliminate service account keys entirely by using Workload Identity (for GKE) or Workload Identity Federation (for external workloads). This removes the need for key management altogether. Cron-based rotation is error-prone. IAM Conditions don't apply to keys. Cloud KMS doesn't manage SA keys.

questions.question: You are designing a network architecture that requires private connectivity from your VPC to Google APIs like Cloud Storage and BigQuery without going over the public internet. Which feature should you use?
options: Private Google Access
Cloud NAT
VPN Gateway
Cloud Interconnect
questions.explanation: Private Google Access allows VMs without external IP addresses to reach Google APIs and services using internal IP addresses through Google's private network. Cloud NAT provides outbound internet access. VPN connects on-premises networks. Cloud Interconnect provides dedicated connectivity to Google Cloud.

questions.question: An organization wants to centralize network security policy enforcement across all VPCs in their Google Cloud organization. Which feature should they use?
options: Hierarchical Firewall Policies
VPC firewall rules in each project
Cloud Armor security policies
Network tags on individual VMs
questions.explanation: Hierarchical Firewall Policies allow organization or folder-level administrators to enforce firewall rules across all VPCs in the hierarchy. Individual VPC rules are project-specific. Cloud Armor protects web applications. Network tags are VM-level labels for firewall targeting.

questions.question: A company must comply with PCI DSS and needs to segment their cardholder data environment (CDE) from the rest of their Google Cloud infrastructure. Which combination provides the strongest segmentation?
options: Dedicated project for CDE with VPC Service Controls, restricted APIs, and organization policies
Separate subnet in the same VPC with firewall rules
Different Kubernetes namespaces in a shared GKE cluster
IAM role bindings restricting access to CDE resources
questions.explanation: PCI DSS requires strong segmentation of the cardholder data environment. A dedicated project provides resource isolation, VPC Service Controls create an API-level perimeter, and organization policies enforce compliance constraints. Subnets with firewalls are insufficient for PCI. Namespaces offer soft isolation. IAM alone doesn't provide network segmentation.

questions.question: Which Google Cloud feature encrypts data at rest by default without any customer configuration?
options: Google-managed encryption keys (default encryption at rest)
Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK)
Customer-Supplied Encryption Keys (CSEK)
Client-side encryption
questions.explanation: Google Cloud encrypts all data at rest by default using Google-managed encryption keys. No customer configuration is required. CMEK, CSEK, and client-side encryption require additional customer setup and management.

questions.question: You need to implement Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) to control access to a web application running on App Engine. What does IAP verify before allowing access?
options: The user's identity and the IAP access policy (IAM permissions) for the resource
Only the user's IP address against an allowlist
The user's VPN connection status
The SSL certificate presented by the user's browser
questions.explanation: Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) verifies the user's identity through Google Sign-In and checks IAP-specific IAM permissions to determine if the user is authorized to access the resource. It provides a zero-trust access model based on identity, not network location.

questions.question: An organization needs to implement defense in depth for their data. They want encryption at rest, encryption in transit, and encryption during processing. Which combination addresses all three?
options: CMEK for at-rest encryption, TLS for in-transit encryption, and Confidential VMs for processing encryption
Default encryption for at rest, HTTPS for transit, and VPC firewall rules for processing
CSEK for all three layers
Cloud KMS for at-rest and in-transit, with application-level encryption for processing
questions.explanation: CMEK provides customer-controlled encryption at rest, TLS/HTTPS encrypts data in transit, and Confidential VMs use hardware-based memory encryption to protect data during processing. Firewall rules are not encryption. CSEK doesn't cover transit or processing. Cloud KMS doesn't encrypt in transit or during processing directly.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'constraints/iam.disableServiceAccountCreation' organization policy?
options: It prevents users from creating new service accounts in projects under the policy
It disables all existing service accounts
It prevents service accounts from being used for authentication
It removes all service account keys automatically
questions.explanation: This organization policy constraint prevents the creation of new user-managed service accounts in projects where the policy is applied. Existing service accounts continue to function. It doesn't disable existing accounts, prevent authentication, or remove keys.

questions.question: You need to grant a contractor temporary access to a Google Cloud project that automatically expires in 30 days. Which IAM feature should you use?
options: IAM Conditions with a time-based expression setting an expiry timestamp
Grant access and set a calendar reminder to revoke it
Create a custom role that only lasts 30 days
Use a separate project that will be deleted after 30 days
questions.explanation: IAM Conditions support time-based expressions (request.time < timestamp) that automatically expire the role binding after the specified date. Calendar reminders are manual and error-prone. Custom roles don't have expiration. Deleting a project is extreme.

questions.question: A company discovers that a service account key was accidentally committed to a public GitHub repository. What should be their IMMEDIATE first action?
options: Disable or delete the compromised service account key immediately
Audit all Cloud Audit Logs for unauthorized access
Rotate the service account key
Enable VPC Service Controls
questions.explanation: The immediate priority is to disable or delete the compromised key to prevent unauthorized access. Rotating (creating a new key and deleting the old) takes longer. Auditing logs and enabling VPC-SC are important follow-up steps but should not delay key revocation.

questions.question: Which Google Cloud service provides web application firewall (WAF) capabilities to protect against OWASP Top 10 threats?
options: Cloud Armor
Cloud IDS
VPC firewall rules
Security Command Center
questions.explanation: Cloud Armor provides WAF capabilities with preconfigured rules for OWASP Top 10 threats including SQL injection and XSS. It integrates with the global HTTP(S) load balancer. Cloud IDS detects intrusions. VPC firewall rules operate at network layer. Security Command Center monitors security posture.

questions.question: You are designing a zero-trust security architecture for an organization migrating to Google Cloud. Which combination of services implements the core principles of zero trust?
options: Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP), BeyondCorp Enterprise, VPC Service Controls, and Context-Aware Access
VPN with firewall rules and network ACLs
Cloud Armor with IP allowlisting and SSL certificates
Dedicated Interconnect with private VLANs and access control lists
questions.explanation: Zero trust means 'never trust, always verify.' IAP verifies identity per-request, BeyondCorp enables secure access without VPN, VPC Service Controls create API perimeters, and Context-Aware Access evaluates device posture and context. VPN-based approaches trust the network, violating zero-trust principles.

questions.question: Which Google Cloud tool provides recommendations for reducing costs by identifying idle or underutilized resources?
options: Recommender (Active Assist)
Cloud Billing Reports
Cloud Monitoring
Cloud Trace
questions.explanation: Google Cloud Recommender (part of Active Assist) analyzes resource usage and provides actionable recommendations including rightsizing VMs, deleting idle resources, and purchasing committed use discounts. Billing Reports show costs but don't recommend actions. Monitoring tracks metrics. Trace analyzes latency.

questions.question: What is an SLI (Service Level Indicator) in the context of Site Reliability Engineering?
options: A quantitative measure of a specific aspect of service quality, such as latency or availability
A contract between a provider and a customer defining service expectations
A target value or range for a service level measured by an indicator
A budget of allowed downtime based on the service level objective
questions.explanation: An SLI is a quantitative measurement of service quality, such as request latency (p99), error rate, or availability percentage. An SLA is the contract. An SLO is the target. An error budget is the allowed downtime derived from the SLO.

questions.question: A company is spending significantly on Compute Engine. They have predictable workloads that run 24/7 for the next 3 years. Which pricing model offers the LARGEST discount?
options: 3-year Committed Use Discount (CUD)
1-year Committed Use Discount (CUD)
Sustained Use Discount (SUD)
Spot VM pricing
questions.explanation: 3-year Committed Use Discounts offer the largest discount (up to 57% for most machine types) for predictable, long-running workloads. 1-year CUDs offer less discount. SUDs are automatic but smaller. Spot VMs offer large discounts but can be preempted, making them unsuitable for 24/7 workloads.

questions.question: You notice that a BigQuery query is scanning the entire table (2 TB) each time it runs, but it only needs data from the last 7 days. How should you optimize this?
options: Partition the table by date and include a date filter in the WHERE clause
Create a materialized view for the last 7 days
Move the data to Cloud Bigtable for faster scans
Increase the number of BigQuery slots
questions.explanation: Partitioning the table by date and filtering on the partition column in the WHERE clause causes BigQuery to only scan the relevant partitions, dramatically reducing data scanned and cost. Materialized views help but still scan the source table. Bigtable is a different database type. More slots increase parallel processing but don't reduce data scanned.

questions.question: A company's Cloud SQL database is experiencing high CPU utilization during peak hours, causing slow response times. After analysis, you find that 80% of queries are read-only dashboard queries. What is the MOST effective optimization?
options: Create read replicas and direct dashboard queries to the replicas
Upgrade to a larger machine type with more vCPUs
Enable Cloud SQL query insights and add missing indexes
Migrate to Cloud Spanner for better horizontal scaling
questions.explanation: Read replicas offload read-heavy workloads from the primary instance, directly addressing the 80% read-only dashboard queries. Upgrading is more expensive. Query insights and indexes may help but don't solve the fundamental read load distribution. Migrating to Spanner is expensive and unnecessary.

questions.question: What is an error budget in SRE practice?
options: The acceptable amount of service unreliability allowed by the SLO, calculated as 1 minus the SLO
The budget allocated for fixing production bugs
The maximum number of errors before a service is shut down
The cost of downtime calculated in dollars
questions.explanation: An error budget is derived from the SLO: if the SLO is 99.9% availability, the error budget is 0.1% (about 43 minutes per month). It quantifies acceptable unreliability and helps balance velocity (feature releases) with reliability.

questions.question: A team is using Cloud Monitoring to track application performance. They want to be alerted when the 95th percentile request latency exceeds 500ms for more than 5 minutes. Which alerting configuration should they use?
options: Metric-based alerting policy with a threshold condition on the latency distribution metric using p95 aggregation
Log-based metric that counts requests over 500ms
Uptime check monitoring the application endpoint
Custom dashboard with manual monitoring during business hours
questions.explanation: A metric-based alerting policy can monitor the p95 latency distribution metric and trigger when it exceeds 500ms for the specified duration. Log-based metrics are less efficient for latency percentiles. Uptime checks test availability, not latency percentiles. Manual monitoring is not scalable.

questions.question: You are tasked with optimizing a GKE cluster's cost. Pods are using only 30% of their requested CPU and memory on average. Which optimization strategy addresses this MOST effectively?
options: Enable Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) to automatically right-size pod resource requests based on actual usage
Reduce the number of nodes in the cluster manually
Switch all node pools to Spot VMs
Disable autoscaling and use a fixed number of nodes
questions.explanation: VPA monitors actual resource usage and automatically adjusts pod resource requests and limits, directly addressing the over-provisioning problem. Reducing nodes manually risks pod eviction. Spot VMs reduce per-node cost but don't fix over-provisioning. Disabling autoscaling reduces flexibility.

questions.question: Which Cloud Monitoring feature allows you to aggregate and visualize metrics from multiple Google Cloud projects in a single view?
options: Monitoring scoping projects (metrics scope)
Custom dashboards in each project
Cloud Logging log router
BigQuery data transfer
questions.explanation: Monitoring scoping projects (metrics scope) allow you to view and manage metrics from multiple monitored projects in a single pane. Custom dashboards are project-specific. Log router routes logs, not metrics. BigQuery transfers are for data, not metrics visualization.

questions.question: What does the Google Cloud Billing Export to BigQuery provide?
options: Detailed billing data that can be analyzed with SQL queries for cost optimization
Real-time alerts when spending exceeds a budget
Automatic cost reduction recommendations
A visual dashboard of current month spending
questions.explanation: Billing Export to BigQuery streams detailed billing data into a BigQuery dataset, enabling custom SQL analysis, cost allocation, trend analysis, and integration with BI tools. Alerts come from billing budgets. Recommendations come from Recommender. Visual dashboards are in the Billing Console.

questions.question: A company is running a Dataflow streaming pipeline that processes 10,000 events per second. During peak hours (2x traffic), the pipeline develops significant lag. What is the MOST appropriate optimization?
options: Enable Dataflow autoscaling to automatically add workers during peak traffic
Pre-provision double the workers to handle peak load at all times
Reduce the window duration to process fewer events per window
Switch from streaming to batch processing
questions.explanation: Dataflow autoscaling dynamically adjusts the number of workers based on pipeline lag, handling traffic spikes efficiently without over-provisioning during normal hours. Pre-provisioning wastes money during off-peak. Reducing window duration doesn't address throughput. Switching to batch loses real-time processing.

questions.question: You want to label all resources in a project for cost allocation by department. Which Google Cloud feature should you use?
options: Resource labels
Network tags
IAM custom roles
Organization policies
questions.explanation: Resource labels are key-value pairs that can be attached to most Google Cloud resources. They appear in billing exports, enabling cost allocation by department, team, environment, etc. Network tags are for firewall rules. IAM roles control access. Organization policies enforce constraints.

questions.question: A company has a multi-region Cloud Spanner database costing $20,000/month. They realize that 90% of reads could tolerate stale data up to 15 seconds old. How can they optimize costs?
options: Use stale reads (bounded staleness) for the 90% of queries that can tolerate eventual consistency to reduce read load on leader replicas
Downgrade to a single-region instance
Migrate to Cloud SQL with read replicas
Reduce the number of nodes below the minimum recommended
questions.explanation: Stale reads in Cloud Spanner can be served by any replica, not just the leader, distributing read load more efficiently and potentially allowing fewer nodes. This preserves multi-region availability while reducing costs. Downgrading loses availability. Cloud SQL migration is a major undertaking. Under-provisioning causes performance issues.

questions.question: Which Google Cloud service helps you trace requests across distributed microservices to identify latency bottlenecks?
options: Cloud Trace
Cloud Profiler
Cloud Debugger
Error Reporting
questions.explanation: Cloud Trace collects latency data from applications and displays it as traces, showing how requests propagate through microservices and identifying bottlenecks. Cloud Profiler analyzes CPU and memory. Cloud Debugger inspects code state. Error Reporting aggregates errors.

questions.question: A company is evaluating the total cost of ownership (TCO) for migrating to Google Cloud. Which costs should be included beyond the cloud compute and storage charges?
options: Migration costs, retraining staff, refactoring applications, network egress, and operational changes
Only the monthly cloud service bills
Only the one-time migration project cost
Only the savings from decommissioning on-premises hardware
questions.explanation: TCO analysis must include all costs: migration effort, staff training, application refactoring, ongoing network egress charges, operational process changes, and the cloud service costs. Only considering monthly bills, one-time costs, or savings gives an incomplete picture.

questions.question: You manage a GKE cluster with cluster autoscaler enabled. You notice that the autoscaler is slow to scale up when traffic spikes occur, causing temporary performance degradation. What is the MOST effective improvement?
options: Configure over-provisioning with a low-priority 'pause' pod that reserves extra capacity and is evicted when real pods need resources
Disable autoscaler and manually maintain extra nodes
Increase the maximum number of nodes in the autoscaler configuration
Switch to Horizontal Pod Autoscaler only, without cluster autoscaling
questions.explanation: Over-provisioning with low-priority pause pods reserves headroom so new pods can be scheduled immediately on existing nodes while the autoscaler provisions new nodes. Disabling autoscaler wastes resources. Increasing max nodes doesn't speed up scaling. HPA without cluster autoscaling can't add capacity.

questions.question: Which BigQuery feature reduces query costs by limiting the amount of data processed per query?
options: Maximum bytes billed setting
BigQuery BI Engine
BigQuery Omni
Authorized views
questions.explanation: The maximum bytes billed setting allows you to set a limit on the amount of data a query can scan. If the query would exceed this limit, it fails instead of running, preventing unexpectedly expensive queries. BI Engine caches data. Omni queries other clouds. Authorized views control access.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Cloud Billing budgets and alerts?
options: To notify stakeholders when spending approaches or exceeds a defined threshold
To automatically stop all Google Cloud services when a budget is exceeded
To provide discounts when spending stays under budget
To restrict which services can be used within a project
questions.explanation: Billing budgets send alerts (email, Pub/Sub) when spending approaches or exceeds configured thresholds (e.g., 50%, 75%, 100% of budget). They don't automatically stop services, provide discounts, or restrict service usage by default. Automatic shutdown requires additional Cloud Functions integration.

questions.question: A company runs Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL and sees that connection pooling at the application level is insufficient during traffic spikes, exhausting the database's maximum connection limit. Which Google Cloud feature addresses this?
options: Cloud SQL Auth Proxy with connection pooling or AlloyDB Omni with built-in connection pooling
Increase the max_connections parameter to 10,000
Deploy multiple Cloud SQL instances behind a load balancer
Switch to Firestore which has no connection limits
questions.explanation: Cloud SQL Auth Proxy supports connection pooling to multiplex many application connections over fewer database connections. AlloyDB also has built-in connection pooling. Simply increasing max_connections can degrade performance. Load balancing Cloud SQL is not natively supported. Firestore is a different database type.

questions.question: Which practice helps a team decide whether to launch a new feature or focus on reliability improvements?
options: Error budget policy — if the error budget is depleted, halt feature releases and focus on reliability
Always prioritize features over reliability
Conduct a weekly vote among engineers
Wait until customers complain about reliability before acting
questions.explanation: The error budget policy is an SRE practice where the remaining error budget determines whether to release features (budget available) or focus on reliability (budget depleted). This provides an objective, data-driven approach to balancing velocity and reliability.

questions.question: Which Google Cloud service allows you to analyze resource usage and get actionable recommendations for rightsizing VMs?
options: Compute Engine Recommender
Cloud Scheduler
Cloud Functions
Cloud Endpoints
questions.explanation: Compute Engine Recommender analyzes VM utilization data and provides specific rightsizing recommendations (resize to smaller type, change machine family, etc.) to optimize costs. Cloud Scheduler triggers jobs. Cloud Functions runs code. Cloud Endpoints manages APIs.

questions.question: A team wants to visualize how long each phase of a request takes as it moves through their microservices. Which Google Cloud feature provides this capability?
options: Cloud Trace with distributed tracing spans
Cloud Monitoring dashboards with uptime checks
Cloud Logging with structured log queries
Error Reporting with stack trace analysis
questions.explanation: Cloud Trace provides distributed tracing, showing spans for each phase of a request as it moves through microservices. This waterfall view identifies latency bottlenecks across services. Monitoring shows metrics but not per-request traces. Logging shows individual events. Error Reporting focuses on errors.

questions.question: A company is evaluating moving from a self-managed Kubernetes cluster on Compute Engine to GKE Autopilot. Which of the following is a valid cost consideration?
options: Autopilot charges per pod resource request rather than per node, potentially saving money by eliminating unused node capacity
Autopilot is always more expensive than Standard mode
Autopilot eliminates all compute charges by using a free tier
Autopilot requires purchasing Committed Use Discounts for the control plane
questions.explanation: GKE Autopilot bills per pod resource request (CPU, memory, ephemeral storage) rather than per node, so you don't pay for unused capacity on nodes. This can reduce costs when workloads don't fully utilize node resources. Autopilot is not always more expensive and there is no free tier.

questions.question: You need to set an SLO for an API service. The business requires 99.9% availability and p99 latency under 200ms. What would be the monthly error budget for availability?
options: Approximately 43 minutes of downtime per month
Approximately 4.3 hours of downtime per month
Approximately 7.3 hours of downtime per month
Approximately 8.7 hours of downtime per month
questions.explanation: For 99.9% availability: 0.1% of a 30-day month = 0.001 x 30 x 24 x 60 = 43.2 minutes. This is the error budget — the maximum allowed downtime per month while meeting the SLO.

questions.question: A company's Cloud Storage costs have tripled in the past quarter. Analysis shows most objects are rarely accessed after 30 days. Which strategy will provide the MOST significant cost reduction?
options: Implement Object Lifecycle Management to automatically transition objects to Nearline after 30 days and Coldline after 90 days
Enable Object Versioning to track changes
Move all data to a single-region bucket
Compress all objects before uploading
questions.explanation: Object Lifecycle Management automatically transitions objects to cheaper storage classes based on age. Moving to Nearline (30 days) and Coldline (90 days) significantly reduces storage costs for infrequently accessed data. Versioning increases costs. Single-region saves less. Compression helps but doesn't address storage class pricing.

questions.question: Which Google Cloud tool helps you estimate the cost of running specific workloads before deployment?
options: Google Cloud Pricing Calculator
Cloud Billing Reports
Cloud Monitoring
Recommender
questions.explanation: The Google Cloud Pricing Calculator lets you estimate costs for specific service configurations before deploying resources. Billing Reports show actual historical costs. Cloud Monitoring tracks metrics. Recommender provides optimization suggestions for existing resources.

questions.question: A development team is performing load testing on their application and needs to understand CPU and memory usage patterns at the code level. Which Google Cloud service should they use?
options: Cloud Profiler
Cloud Trace
Cloud Monitoring
Cloud Logging
questions.explanation: Cloud Profiler continuously collects CPU and memory usage data at the code level, identifying which functions consume the most resources. Cloud Trace shows request latency. Cloud Monitoring tracks infrastructure metrics. Cloud Logging records log entries.

questions.question: Which Google Cloud service provides a fully managed CI/CD platform for building, testing, and deploying applications?
options: Cloud Build
Cloud Source Repositories
Artifact Registry
Cloud Deploy
questions.explanation: Cloud Build is a fully managed CI/CD service that executes builds on Google Cloud infrastructure. It supports building from source repositories, running tests, and creating container images. Cloud Source Repositories hosts code. Artifact Registry stores artifacts. Cloud Deploy handles continuous delivery.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Artifact Registry in a CI/CD pipeline?
options: To store and manage container images, language packages, and OS packages
To execute build steps and run tests
To deploy applications to production environments
To monitor application performance after deployment
questions.explanation: Artifact Registry is a universal package manager that stores container images (Docker), language packages (npm, Maven, Python), and OS packages. It integrates with Cloud Build for CI/CD pipelines. Building is done by Cloud Build. Deployment by Cloud Deploy. Monitoring by Cloud Monitoring.

questions.question: You need to implement a continuous deployment pipeline that promotes a container image through dev, staging, and production environments with approval gates between stages. Which service should you use?
options: Cloud Deploy
Cloud Build triggers
Cloud Scheduler with Cloud Functions
kubectl apply with manual scripts
questions.explanation: Cloud Deploy provides a managed continuous delivery service with delivery pipelines that define promotion sequences across environments (dev, staging, prod) with approval gates. Cloud Build handles CI but not multi-stage delivery. Cloud Scheduler/Functions is a workaround. Manual kubectl is not automated.

questions.question: Which Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tool is maintained by Google and uses a declarative language specific to Google Cloud resources?
options: Google Cloud Deployment Manager
Terraform
Pulumi
Ansible
questions.explanation: Cloud Deployment Manager is Google's native IaC tool using YAML/Jinja2/Python templates to define Google Cloud resources declaratively. Terraform is by HashiCorp and is multi-cloud. Pulumi uses general-purpose programming languages. Ansible is configuration management.

questions.question: You are managing Terraform state for a large organization with multiple teams working on different Google Cloud projects. How should you configure Terraform state to prevent conflicts and ensure consistency?
options: Use a remote backend (Cloud Storage) with state locking enabled and separate state files per team/environment
Store state files in each developer's local machine
Commit state files to the Git repository
Use a single shared state file for all teams and environments
questions.explanation: A remote backend (Cloud Storage) provides centralized state storage, state locking prevents concurrent modifications, and separate state files per team/environment reduce blast radius. Local state has no collaboration. Git state causes conflicts. A single shared state increases risk of cross-team interference.

questions.question: Which command-line tool is used to interact with Google Cloud services for managing resources, deploying applications, and running administrative commands?
options: gcloud CLI
gsutil
kubectl
bq
questions.explanation: The gcloud CLI is the primary command-line tool for Google Cloud, supporting resource management across most services. gsutil is specifically for Cloud Storage. kubectl is for Kubernetes. bq is for BigQuery.

questions.question: A team wants to ensure that all infrastructure changes go through code review before being applied to production. Which workflow should they implement?
options: Store Terraform configurations in a Git repository with pull request reviews, and use Cloud Build to run terraform plan on PRs and terraform apply on merge to main
Allow developers to run terraform apply from their local machines
Use the Cloud Console for all infrastructure changes
Have one administrator make all changes using gcloud commands
questions.explanation: GitOps workflow with Terraform in Git, PR reviews for code changes, automated plan on PRs (for review), and automated apply on merge ensures all changes are reviewed, tracked, and reproducible. Local apply bypasses review. Console changes are not tracked in code. Single admin is a bottleneck and SPOF.

questions.question: You need to set up a Cloud Build pipeline that builds a container image, runs integration tests against a temporary Cloud SQL database, and pushes the image to Artifact Registry only if tests pass. How should you structure the build?
options: Use a multi-step cloudbuild.yaml with steps for building the image, starting a Cloud SQL proxy sidecar, running tests, and conditionally pushing to Artifact Registry
Create separate Cloud Build triggers for each step with manual execution between them
Use Cloud Build for the image build only and Jenkins for testing and pushing
Build and push the image first, then run tests against the deployed image in production
questions.explanation: Cloud Build supports multi-step builds in a single cloudbuild.yaml where steps execute sequentially. This allows building, testing with Cloud SQL proxy, and conditionally pushing in an atomic pipeline. Separate triggers require coordination. Jenkins adds complexity. Testing in production is risky.

questions.question: Which Google Cloud service allows you to manage Kubernetes resources using Terraform-like declarative YAML manifests that are reconciled continuously?
options: Config Connector
Cloud Deploy
Deployment Manager
Cloud Build
questions.explanation: Config Connector is a Kubernetes add-on that maps Google Cloud resources to Kubernetes objects, allowing you to manage cloud resources using kubectl and declarative YAML manifests with continuous reconciliation. Cloud Deploy handles app delivery. Deployment Manager uses its own templates. Cloud Build runs CI/CD.

questions.question: What is Cloud Shell in Google Cloud?
options: A browser-based terminal with pre-installed tools like gcloud, kubectl, and Terraform
A managed SSH service for connecting to VMs
A command-line tool installed on your local machine
A service for running shell scripts on a schedule
questions.explanation: Cloud Shell provides a browser-based terminal environment with a temporary VM that comes pre-installed with gcloud, kubectl, Terraform, and other development tools. It includes 5 GB of persistent home directory storage. It's not SSH to your VMs, not local software, and not a scheduler.

questions.question: A company uses Terraform to manage their Google Cloud infrastructure. A developer accidentally deletes a Cloud SQL production database using terraform destroy. How should they prevent this in the future?
options: Enable the prevent_destroy lifecycle rule on critical resources and use IAM to restrict who can run terraform destroy
Only use Cloud Console for managing production resources
Remove Terraform from the deployment pipeline entirely
Create daily manual backups of the Terraform state file
