questions.explanation: The prevent_destroy lifecycle rule in Terraform causes an error if someone tries to destroy the protected resource. Combined with IAM restrictions on who can run destructive operations, this provides strong protection. Abandoning Terraform loses IaC benefits. State file backups don't prevent destruction.

questions.question: You need to set up a CI/CD pipeline that automatically deploys to Cloud Run whenever code is pushed to the main branch of a Cloud Source Repository. Which approach requires the LEAST configuration?
options: Cloud Run continuous deployment feature connected directly to the source repository
Cloud Build trigger with a custom cloudbuild.yaml and gcloud run deploy step
Jenkins pipeline with Google Cloud plugin
GitHub Actions with google-github-actions/deploy-cloudrun
questions.explanation: Cloud Run's built-in continuous deployment feature can be connected directly to a source repository, automatically building and deploying on push with minimal configuration. Cloud Build triggers require custom YAML. Jenkins and GitHub Actions require additional setup.

questions.question: A team is using Cloud Build with Terraform. They want to implement a workflow where terraform plan output is added as a comment on the pull request for review before merging and applying. Which approach implements this?
options: Cloud Build trigger on PR events that runs terraform plan, captures output, and posts it as a PR comment using the GitHub/Cloud Source API
Email the terraform plan output to the team lead manually
Run terraform plan locally and paste the output in the PR description
Skip the plan step and go directly to apply on merge
questions.explanation: Automating terraform plan on PR events and posting the output as a comment enables reviewers to see exactly what infrastructure changes will occur. This is a GitOps best practice. Manual steps are error-prone. Skipping plan is risky.

questions.question: Which Google Cloud service provides a managed API gateway that handles authentication, rate limiting, and monitoring for backend APIs?
options: Apigee
Cloud Endpoints
Cloud Load Balancing
Cloud CDN
questions.explanation: Apigee is Google Cloud's full-featured API management platform that provides authentication, rate limiting, analytics, monetization, and monitoring for APIs. Cloud Endpoints is simpler API management. Load Balancing distributes traffic. Cloud CDN caches content.

questions.question: What is the Google Cloud Client Library used for?
options: Interacting with Google Cloud services programmatically from application code in supported languages
Managing Google Cloud billing and invoices
Deploying infrastructure using YAML templates
Monitoring application performance metrics
questions.explanation: Google Cloud Client Libraries provide idiomatic, language-specific interfaces for interacting with Google Cloud services from application code (Python, Java, Go, Node.js, etc.). They handle authentication, retries, and pagination. Billing has its own UI. YAML templates are for Deployment Manager. Monitoring has its own tools.

questions.question: You are implementing a GitOps workflow for GKE using Config Sync. What is the primary benefit of this approach over manual kubectl apply?
options: Config Sync continuously reconciles the desired state in Git with the actual cluster state, automatically correcting drift
Config Sync is faster at applying manifests
Config Sync eliminates the need for YAML files
Config Sync replaces the need for a container registry
questions.explanation: Config Sync implements GitOps by continuously syncing the desired state from a Git repository to GKE clusters. If someone makes a manual change (drift), Config Sync automatically reverts it. This ensures the Git repository is always the source of truth. Speed, YAML, and registries are not the primary benefit.

questions.question: A team needs to manage Terraform modules that are reused across multiple projects. Where should they store these shared modules?
options: In a dedicated Git repository (or Terraform module registry) referenced by all consuming projects
Copy-pasted into each project's Terraform directory
In a shared Cloud Storage bucket as zip files
Inline in each project's main.tf file
questions.explanation: Storing shared Terraform modules in a dedicated Git repository (or using Google Cloud's Terraform module registry) allows version-controlled, centralized module management. Projects reference modules by Git URL and version tag. Copy-pasting creates maintenance nightmares. Storage buckets lose version control. Inline modules are not reusable.

questions.question: Which Google Cloud feature allows you to preview infrastructure changes before actually creating or modifying resources?
options: Deployment Manager preview mode or Terraform plan
Cloud Console resource creation wizard
gcloud --dry-run flag
Cloud Shell code editor
questions.explanation: Deployment Manager's preview mode and Terraform's plan command both show what changes will be made without actually applying them. This allows review before execution. Console wizards create resources immediately. gcloud doesn't have a universal dry-run flag. Cloud Shell editor just edits files.

questions.question: What is the difference between RPO and RTO in disaster recovery planning?
options: RPO is the maximum acceptable data loss measured in time, while RTO is the maximum acceptable downtime before service is restored
RPO measures recovery speed and RTO measures data backup frequency
RPO is the recovery point for databases and RTO is for applications only
RPO and RTO are the same metric measured in different units
questions.explanation: Recovery Point Objective (RPO) defines the maximum acceptable amount of data loss (e.g., 1 hour means you can lose up to 1 hour of data). Recovery Time Objective (RTO) defines the maximum acceptable time to restore service after a disaster.

questions.question: Which deployment strategy runs two identical production environments and switches traffic between them?
options: Blue-green deployment
Canary deployment
Rolling update
Recreate deployment
questions.explanation: Blue-green deployment maintains two identical environments (blue = current, green = new). After deploying and testing the new version on green, traffic is switched from blue to green. This allows instant rollback by switching back. Canary gradually shifts traffic. Rolling updates replace instances incrementally. Recreate stops all then starts all.

questions.question: A company needs a disaster recovery solution for their web application with an RTO of 1 hour and RPO of 15 minutes. Which DR strategy is MOST cost-effective while meeting these requirements?
options: Warm standby with asynchronous replication and pre-provisioned scaled-down infrastructure
Hot standby with synchronous replication running at full production scale
Cold standby with daily backups to Cloud Storage
Backup and restore from regional persistent disk snapshots
questions.explanation: Warm standby provides a scaled-down replica that can be scaled up quickly (meeting 1-hour RTO) with asynchronous replication (meeting 15-minute RPO). Hot standby would meet the requirements but costs much more. Cold standby and backup/restore would have RPO measured in hours, not minutes.

questions.question: You are running a critical application on a managed instance group. You want to ensure that unhealthy VMs are automatically replaced. Which feature should you configure?
options: Autohealing with a health check
Autoscaling with CPU utilization target
Load balancing with session affinity
Instance template with startup scripts
questions.explanation: Autohealing uses health checks to detect unhealthy VMs and automatically recreates them from the instance template. Autoscaling adjusts the number of instances based on load. Load balancing distributes traffic. Startup scripts run during VM initialization.

questions.question: An online banking application requires 99.99% availability SLA. The application uses Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL. Which architecture meets this requirement?
options: Cloud SQL HA instance with cross-region read replicas, automated backups, and application-level retry logic with connection pooling
Single Cloud SQL instance with automated backups
Cloud SQL HA instance in a single region only
Self-managed PostgreSQL on Compute Engine with manual failover scripts
questions.explanation: Cloud SQL HA provides 99.95% SLA within a region. Adding cross-region replicas, automated backups, and application-level retry logic provides the additional resilience needed to approach 99.99%. A single instance lacks HA. Single-region HA alone is 99.95%. Self-managed databases require more operational overhead.

questions.question: Which Google Cloud support tier provides a 15-minute initial response time for P1 (Critical Impact) issues?
options: Premium Support
Enhanced Support
Standard Support
Basic Support
questions.explanation: Premium Support provides a 15-minute initial response time for P1 critical impact issues, along with a Technical Account Manager and proactive support. Enhanced offers 1-hour P1 response. Standard offers 4-hour P1 response. Basic has no guaranteed response time.

questions.question: You need to implement a deployment strategy for a GKE application that sends 5% of traffic to the new version for testing before a full rollout. Which strategy should you use?
options: Canary deployment
Blue-green deployment
Rolling update
A/B testing with feature flags
questions.explanation: Canary deployment sends a small percentage of traffic (e.g., 5%) to the new version while the majority still goes to the old version. If metrics are good, traffic gradually shifts. Blue-green switches all traffic at once. Rolling updates replace pods incrementally. A/B testing tests features, not versions.

questions.question: A company runs a stateful application on GKE that writes to local persistent volumes. They need to implement a disaster recovery strategy with RPO of 1 hour across regions. What should they implement?
options: Schedule periodic snapshots of persistent disks and use Backup for GKE to replicate to a secondary region
Use emptyDir volumes that are automatically replicated
Rely on GKE's built-in cross-region replication for persistent volumes
Use only ConfigMaps for all stateful data
questions.explanation: Backup for GKE provides managed backup and restore capabilities including cross-region replication of persistent volume snapshots. Scheduled hourly snapshots meet the 1-hour RPO. emptyDir is ephemeral. GKE doesn't natively replicate PVs across regions. ConfigMaps are not for application data.

questions.question: During a production incident, you need to quickly determine which recent change caused the issue. Which Google Cloud feature helps with this?
options: Cloud Audit Logs showing recent API calls that modified resources
Cloud Billing showing recent cost changes
Cloud DNS logs showing domain resolution
Cloud CDN logs showing cache hit rates
questions.explanation: Cloud Audit Logs (Admin Activity logs) record all API calls that modify resources, allowing you to identify recent changes that may have caused the incident. Billing changes don't show resource modifications. DNS and CDN logs are not relevant to infrastructure changes.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a health check in Google Cloud load balancing?
options: To determine whether backend instances are healthy and able to receive traffic
To check the security posture of backend instances
To verify SSL certificate validity
To measure the bandwidth capacity of backend instances
questions.explanation: Health checks periodically probe backend instances to determine if they are healthy and able to serve traffic. Unhealthy instances are removed from the load balancer's rotation until they recover. They don't check security posture, SSL certificates, or bandwidth.

questions.question: You are designing a multi-region active-active architecture for a global application. How should you handle database writes to avoid conflicts?
options: Use Cloud Spanner with its distributed consensus protocol for globally consistent writes
Use Cloud SQL with master-master replication across regions
Use Firestore with last-writer-wins conflict resolution
Use BigQuery with streaming inserts from each region
questions.explanation: Cloud Spanner uses the TrueTime API and distributed consensus (Paxos) to provide globally consistent, strongly ordered writes across regions, eliminating write conflicts. Cloud SQL master-master replication can cause conflicts. Firestore last-writer-wins can lose data. BigQuery is not designed for OLTP writes.

questions.question: A team is implementing incident management practices. Which Google Cloud tool provides automated alerting when metrics cross defined thresholds?
options: Cloud Monitoring alerting policies
Cloud Logging exclusion filters
Cloud Trace span analysis
Cloud Profiler CPU reports
questions.explanation: Cloud Monitoring alerting policies trigger notifications (email, SMS, PagerDuty, Pub/Sub) when metrics cross defined thresholds or conditions are met. Logging exclusion filters remove log entries. Trace analyzes latency. Profiler analyzes code performance.

questions.question: An application needs to handle a scenario where an entire Google Cloud region becomes unavailable. The application uses Cloud SQL for its database. What is the MINIMUM setup required for cross-region database failover?
options: Cloud SQL cross-region read replica that can be promoted to primary in the event of a regional failure
Cloud SQL HA configuration (within a single region)
Cloud SQL automated backups stored in a multi-region Cloud Storage bucket
Cloud SQL with Binary Logging enabled
questions.explanation: A cross-region read replica can be promoted to become the new primary instance if the primary's region goes down. HA configuration protects against zone failures, not region failures. Backups require restore time (longer RTO). Binary logging alone doesn't provide failover.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a rolling update deployment strategy in GKE?
options: To gradually replace old pods with new pods while maintaining a minimum number of available pods
To deploy all new pods at once and delete all old pods simultaneously
To send a percentage of traffic to the new version while keeping the old version running
To deploy to a separate environment and switch DNS
questions.explanation: Rolling updates incrementally replace old pods with new pods, configured by maxUnavailable and maxSurge parameters. This maintains availability throughout the deployment. Replacing all at once is 'Recreate.' Percentage-based traffic is Canary. DNS switching is Blue-Green.

questions.question: After a production outage, the team wants to prevent the same issue from happening again. Which SRE practice should they follow?
options: Conduct a blameless postmortem documenting the incident timeline, root cause, and preventive action items
Identify and discipline the engineer who caused the outage
Add more monitoring alerts without analyzing the root cause
Immediately deploy a fix and move on to new features
questions.explanation: Blameless postmortems are a core SRE practice that focus on understanding what happened, why, and how to prevent recurrence without blaming individuals. This fosters a culture of learning. Blame discourages reporting. More alerts without analysis causes noise. Moving on without analysis risks recurrence.

questions.question: A critical application has an SLO of 99.95% availability, which translates to approximately 22 minutes of downtime per month. The team consumed 15 minutes of error budget this month due to a deployment issue. What should the team do?
options: Proceed cautiously with remaining releases, implement stricter deployment testing, and monitor the remaining 7-minute error budget closely
Freeze all deployments for the rest of the month
Ignore the error budget consumption and continue as normal
Reduce the SLO to 99.9% to give more error budget
questions.explanation: With 7 minutes remaining, the team should proceed cautiously, improve deployment testing to avoid consuming more budget, and monitor closely. A complete freeze is too extreme with budget remaining. Ignoring error budget defeats its purpose. Reducing SLO to accommodate failures is the wrong approach.

questions.question: Which GKE feature automatically restarts failed containers and reschedules pods on healthy nodes?
options: Kubernetes built-in self-healing (liveness probes, restart policies, and the scheduler)
GKE node auto-repair
Cloud Monitoring alerts
Managed Instance Group autohealing
questions.explanation: Kubernetes natively provides self-healing: liveness probes detect unhealthy containers and restart them, the scheduler reschedules pods when nodes fail, and restart policies define container restart behavior. Node auto-repair fixes node-level issues. Monitoring generates alerts. MIG autohealing is for VMs, not pods.

questions.question: Which Google Cloud service aggregates and displays application errors with stack traces, making it easy to identify and prioritize issues?
options: Error Reporting
Cloud Trace
Cloud Profiler
Cloud Monitoring
questions.explanation: Error Reporting automatically groups and displays application errors with stack traces, occurrence counts, and trends. It helps prioritize which errors to fix first. Cloud Trace shows request latency. Cloud Profiler shows CPU/memory usage. Cloud Monitoring tracks metrics.

questions.question: A company wants to implement chaos engineering to test the resilience of their GKE-based application. Which approach is MOST appropriate?
options: Use a chaos engineering tool like Chaos Monkey or Litmus to randomly terminate pods and verify the application gracefully handles failures
Manually delete production VMs during business hours
Turn off the entire GKE cluster to test recovery procedures
Run load tests until the application crashes
questions.explanation: Chaos engineering tools (Chaos Monkey, Litmus, Chaos Mesh) inject controlled failures (pod termination, network delays, resource exhaustion) to verify application resilience. Manual VM deletion is uncontrolled. Shutting down the cluster tests DR, not resilience. Load testing tests capacity, not failure handling.

questions.question: You need to configure automatic scaling for a GKE deployment based on the number of messages in a Pub/Sub subscription. Which autoscaler should you use?
options: Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) with a custom Pub/Sub metric from Stackdriver adapter
Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA)
Cluster Autoscaler
Cloud Run autoscaling
questions.explanation: HPA can scale based on custom metrics, including external metrics like Pub/Sub subscription message count via the Stackdriver adapter. VPA adjusts individual pod resources. Cluster Autoscaler adds/removes nodes, not pods. Cloud Run autoscaling is for Cloud Run, not GKE.

questions.question: During a major incident, multiple teams need to coordinate. The on-call engineer needs to communicate updates, track resolution progress, and maintain a timeline. Which SRE practice addresses this?
options: Structured incident response with defined roles (Incident Commander, Communications Lead, Operations Lead) and a shared war room
Have each team work independently and merge updates later
Send email updates every hour to all stakeholders
Wait until the incident is resolved and then write a summary
questions.explanation: Structured incident response with defined roles ensures clear accountability: the Incident Commander coordinates, the Communications Lead handles stakeholder updates, and the Operations Lead drives technical resolution. This prevents confusion during critical incidents. Independent work causes duplicate effort. Email is too slow. Waiting loses valuable context.

questions.question: Which Google Cloud feature provides automatic repair of unhealthy GKE nodes by recreating them?
options: Node auto-repair
Node auto-upgrade
Pod disruption budgets
Cluster autoscaler
questions.explanation: Node auto-repair monitors node health and automatically recreates nodes that are unhealthy (e.g., not responding to health checks). Node auto-upgrade handles version updates. Pod disruption budgets control voluntary disruptions. Cluster autoscaler manages node count based on demand.

questions.question: A company runs a global application with users in North America, Europe, and Asia. They need to ensure that a complete regional failure does not cause more than 30 seconds of downtime for any user. Which architecture achieves this?
options: Active-active deployment across all three regions with Global HTTP(S) Load Balancer and Cloud Spanner for globally consistent data
Active-passive deployment with the primary in North America and cold standbys in other regions
Single-region deployment with Cloud CDN for global edge caching
Active-active across two regions with manual DNS failover to the third
questions.explanation: Active-active across all three regions with Global Load Balancer provides automatic failover within seconds (no DNS TTL delay). Cloud Spanner ensures data consistency across regions. Active-passive requires manual promotion. Single-region has no regional failover. Manual DNS failover exceeds the 30-second requirement.
